content.description:
A facility for discovering what functions/methods are available on a given symbol (e.g. `api`, `api.viewport`) and navigating to their source code.

## Existing Infrastructure

Hobson already has per-item symbol indexing:

- [symbol-extractor-lib](item://ac0ac0ac-0003-0000-0000-000000000000) parses code items with Acorn and extracts functions, classes, methods, variables, and properties with name, kind, signature, and source location
- [symbol-indexer-watch](item://ac0ac0ac-0004-0000-0000-000000000000) auto-indexes on item create/update, storing results in `item.content._symbols`
- Symbol navigation via `api.navigate(id, { symbol: 'name' })` scrolls to a symbol in code views
- Symbol transclusion via `![](item://id?symbol=fn)` embeds specific symbols in markdown

The gap is **discovery**: there's no way to ask "what's available on this object?" or search for symbols across items without already knowing which item to look at.

## Prerequisite: Module Provenance via WeakMap

For `help()` to link a runtime object back to its source item, [ModuleSystem](item://33333333-4444-0000-0000-000000000000) needs to track provenance. The approach:

- Add a `WeakMap` (`moduleMetadata`) to ModuleSystem's constructor
- In `require()`, after `evaluateCodeItem()` returns, store `moduleMetadata.set(module, { itemId, itemName })`
- `help()` can then call `api.moduleSystem.moduleMetadata.get(obj)` to find the source item

**Why WeakMap and not a property on the module?** ES module namespace objects (what `import()` returns) are non-extensible by spec — you cannot add properties to them. `Object.defineProperty` will always throw. A WeakMap side-channel is the only option, and it has the bonus of automatic garbage collection when modules are evicted from cache.

This gives automatic provenance for every `require()`'d module — no manual metadata, no maintenance burden, works for user-created libraries too.

## Option A: REPL `dir()` / `help()` / `symbols()` Functions

Add introspection functions directly to the REPL API.

- **`dir(obj)`** — Enumerate an object's properties grouped by kind (functions, sub-namespaces, values). E.g. `dir(api)` lists all methods; `dir(api.viewport)` lists viewport methods. Pure runtime reflection via `Object.keys` + `typeof`.
- **`help(obj, 'methodName')`** — Show signature, description, and source item link for a specific function. Checks the module provenance WeakMap first (handles all libraries automatically), special-cases `api` → kernel-core for kernel API methods. Uses `_symbols` for signatures and offers `api.navigate(sourceItem, { symbol })` for source navigation.
- **`symbols(nameOrId)`** — Shortcut to dump the `_symbols` map for a code item. E.g. `symbols('generic-view')` shows all its functions/classes with signatures.

**Pros:** Lightweight, REPL-native, fits the Lisp/Smalltalk-inspired workflow. Immediate value.
**Cons:** Text-only, requires knowing the object name to start exploring.

## Option B: Cross-Item Symbol Search

A library function that queries `_symbols` across all code items:

```js
await symbolSearch('render')
await symbolSearch('render', { kind: 'function' })
```

Returns a list of `{ item, itemName, symbol, kind, signature }` with navigation links. Builds entirely on the existing `_symbols` infrastructure — no new indexing needed, just a query across items.

Could be integrated into the existing item palette as a "search symbols" mode, or be a standalone REPL function.

**Pros:** Answers "where is X defined?" across the whole system. Uses existing data.
**Cons:** Scans all items on each query (could cache). Only finds statically-defined symbols, not dynamically-constructed API surfaces.

## Option C: Symbol Browser View

A dedicated item/view showing:

- A searchable list of all code items with their symbols
- Filter by kind (function, class, method, variable), name pattern, or item
- Click any symbol → `api.navigate(itemId, { symbol })` to jump to source
- Could show the symbol tree for a specific item, or a flat cross-item list

This is the richest option but builds on top of A and B — the data is all there in `_symbols`, it just needs a UI.

**Pros:** Visual, discoverable, searchable, navigable. Fits "everything is an item."
**Cons:** Most implementation work.

## The Kernel API: No Special Treatment Needed

The kernel API is constructed by `createAPI()` in kernel-core. An earlier version of this document proposed a separate "Kernel API Descriptor" — a manually-maintained metadata map to make the API discoverable. On closer inspection, this is unnecessary. The entire API surface is defined as an object literal in a single method in a single item (kernel-core). The methods are all syntactically visible in the source.

Two gaps initially made it seem like special treatment was needed:

1. **Provenance** — the module provenance WeakMap doesn't help since `api` isn't loaded via `require()`. But this is trivially solved: special-case `api` → kernel-core in `help()`. No metadata map needed.
2. **Symbol extraction** — the current symbol extractor finds top-level function/class declarations but not properties of object literals. This is a limitation of the extractor, not the architecture. Enhancing it to walk object literal properties inside `createAPI()` would make all `api.*` methods appear in kernel-core's `_symbols` automatically.

For **descriptions** (params, return types, purpose), the right approach is JSDoc comments on the `createAPI()` methods — the same mechanism that would work for any code item. The symbol extractor can parse these, keeping documentation co-located with source rather than in a separate map that drifts out of sync.

This means the kernel API becomes discoverable through exactly the same mechanisms as everything else: symbol extraction + WeakMap provenance (+ a small special-case for provenance).

## Recommended Path

These options layer naturally:

1. **Start with WeakMap + A** — Add module provenance to ModuleSystem (small change), then `dir()` for quick enumeration, `help()` backed by WeakMap (libraries) + kernel-core special-case (kernel API). Gives immediate REPL-based discoverability with source navigation.
2. **Enhance symbol extractor** — Teach it to extract object literal properties (for `createAPI()`) and parse JSDoc comments. This makes the kernel API appear in `_symbols` like any other code.
3. **Add B** — Cross-item `symbolSearch()` for "where is this function defined?" queries.
4. **Add C** — Symbol browser view when a visual UI is wanted.

## Priority

Medium — the system is usable without this, but discoverability is a real friction point, especially for the kernel API.
